topics.title: 1. Introduction to Ethical Hacking
topics.definition: Ethical hacking is the authorized process of identifying, testing, and reporting vulnerabilities in systems, networks, applications, and infrastructure.
examPoints: Ethical hackers use many of the same techniques as malicious attackers, but they work:
For the purpose of improving security

topics.title: Ethical hacker vs malicious hacker
comparisons: Works with permission: Works without permission
Protects data: Steals, modifies, or destroys data

topics.title: 2. Security Management Concepts and Principles
topics.definition: Security management is the process of planning, implementing, monitoring, and improving controls that protect information assets.

topics.title: Core security management activities
topics.definition: Risk management identifies threats, vulnerabilities, likelihood, and impact.
examPoints: Policies define what users, administrators, and systems are allowed or required to do.
Auditing verifies whether controls are functioning and whether policies are being followed.
comparisons: Preventive: Stops an incident: Firewall, MFA
Detective: Identifies an incident: IDS, log monitoring
Corrective: Repairs damage: Patch, restore backup
Deterrent: Discourages attacks: Warning banner, CCTV

topics.title: 3. Human Side of Information Security
topics.definition: People are often the weakest point in a security system.
examPoints: Even strong technology can be defeated by:

topics.title: Social engineering
topics.definition: Social engineering manipulates people into revealing information or performing unsafe actions.

topics.title: Insider threats
topics.definition: An employee intentionally steals or damages information.
examPoints: An attacker gains control of an employee account.

topics.title: 4. Threats to Information Systems
topics.definition: A threat is anything capable of causing harm to a system or asset.

topics.title: 5. Threat, Vulnerability, Attack, and Risk
comparisons: Exploit: Method used to abuse a weakness
Attack: Actual attempt to cause harm
Risk: Likelihood and impact of harm

topics.title: Active vs passive attacks
comparisons: Alters data or system operation: Observes without alteration
Easier to detect: Harder to detect
Example: Session hijacking: Example: Traffic analysis

topics.title: Internal vs external threats
comparisons: Originates inside organization: Originates outside organization
Employee or contractor: Hacker or criminal group
May already have access: Must first gain access

topics.title: Targeted vs opportunistic
comparisons: Specific victim selected: Any vulnerable system

topics.title: 7. Protecting Information Systems
topics.definition: Protection requires administrative, technical, and physical controls.

topics.title: CIA Triad
topics.definition: Ensures information is accessible only to authorized users.
examPoints: Ensures data remains accurate and unmodified.

topics.title: 9. Mobile and Wireless Security
topics.definition: Mobile devices face unique risks because they are portable, always connected, and often contain sensitive data.

topics.title: Security measures
examPoints: Install apps only from trusted sources

topics.title: Common attack methods
topics.definition: Fake banking pages collect card details.
examPoints: Malicious apps steal card or OTP data.
Attacker gains control of the victim's mobile number.
Card details are captured through malicious readers.
Apps imitate legitimate wallets or gateways.

topics.title: 11. Information Security Management
topics.definition: Information security management provides governance and strategic control over security.

topics.title: ISMS
topics.definition: An Information Security Management System is a structured framework for managing security.

topics.title: 12. Cyber Crime
topics.definition: Cyber crime is unlawful activity involving computers, networks, applications, or the internet.

topics.title: 13. Cyber Crime in the Internet Context
15. Indian Cyber Law Overview
topics.definition: Cyber offences in India are mainly addressed through:

topics.title: Important caution
topics.definition: Legal section numbers and punishments may change through amendments or judicial interpretation. For examinations, follow the exact material taught by the institute.

topics.title: 16. International Cyber Law
topics.definition: Cyber crime often crosses national borders.

topics.title: 17. Obscenity and Pornography on the Internet
examPoints: Security professionals must not access or retain illegal material except through authorized legal procedures.

topics.title: Penetration testing
topics.definition: Attempts to exploit vulnerabilities to determine actual impact.

topics.title: Exploit
topics.definition: Code or technique that abuses a vulnerability.

topics.title: Backdoor
topics.definition: Hidden method of bypassing normal access controls.

topics.title: Proof of concept
topics.definition: Demonstration that a vulnerability can be exploited.

topics.title: Scope
topics.definition: Systems, techniques, and limits approved for testing.

topics.title: Rules of engagement
topics.definition: Conditions under which the test is performed.

topics.title: Scanning
topics.definition: Identifies hosts, ports, services, and vulnerabilities.

topics.title: Exploitation
topics.definition: Uses a vulnerability to gain access.

topics.title: Cleanup
topics.definition: Removes test accounts, payloads, files, and backdoors.

topics.title: Grey hat
topics.definition: May test without authorization, even if claiming good intentions.

topics.title: Script kiddie
topics.definition: Uses tools without deep technical understanding.

topics.title: Hacktivist
topics.definition: Attacks for ideological or political reasons.

topics.title: Insider
topics.definition: Uses legitimate access for unauthorized purposes.

topics.title: State-sponsored attacker
topics.definition: Operates in support of national objectives.

topics.title: Purple Team
topics.definition: Facilitates collaboration between Red and Blue Teams.

topics.title: Grey Team
topics.definition: In some course material, Grey Team may refer to a mixed or hybrid role. In industry, “Purple Team” is the more common term for coordinated red-blue collaboration.

topics.title: 23. Security, Functionality, and Ease-of-Use Triangle
topics.definition: Security controls often affect usability and functionality.
examPoints: The goal is balance, not maximum security at any cost.

topics.title: 26. Footprinting
topics.definition: Footprinting gathers information about a target.

topics.title: Passive footprinting
topics.definition: No direct interaction with the target.

topics.title: 27. Social Engineering
topics.definition: Social engineering attacks people rather than technology.

topics.title: 28. Traceroute in Footprinting
topics.definition: Traceroute identifies the network path between source and destination.
examPoints: Traceroute commonly uses TTL expiration and ICMP Time Exceeded messages.

topics.title: Stealth scan
topics.definition: A general term for scan techniques intended to reduce detection, often referring to SYN scanning.

topics.title: Netcraft
topics.definition: Provides information about websites, hosting, and technologies.

topics.title: 33. Banner Grabbing
topics.definition: Banner grabbing identifies software and versions running on a service.

topics.title: 34. OS Fingerprinting
topics.definition: OS fingerprinting identifies a target operating system.

topics.title: Active fingerprinting
topics.definition: Sends crafted packets and analyzes responses.

topics.title: 35. Proxy Servers in Attacks
topics.definition: A proxy acts as an intermediary.

topics.title: 36. HTTP Tunneling
topics.definition: HTTP tunneling carries non-HTTP communication inside HTTP or HTTPS traffic.

topics.title: 37. IP Spoofing
topics.definition: IP spoofing forges the source IP address.
examPoints: Replies may go to the spoofed system.
It does not automatically provide two-way communication.

topics.title: 38. Enumeration
topics.definition: Enumeration actively extracts detailed information from services.

topics.title: Password spraying
topics.definition: Tries a small number of common passwords against many accounts.

topics.title: Offline cracking
topics.definition: Attempts to recover passwords from captured hashes.

topics.title: 40. Windows Password Cracking
topics.definition: Windows commonly stores password-derived values in forms such as NTLM hashes.

topics.title: SMB redirection
topics.definition: Victim authentication is redirected to an attacker-controlled SMB service.

topics.title: SMB relay
topics.definition: The attacker forwards captured authentication to another service.
examPoints: The attacker may gain access without knowing the password.

topics.title: 42. NetBIOS DoS
topics.definition: NetBIOS services can be abused through malformed or excessive requests.

topics.title: Passive online attack
topics.definition: Captures credentials without directly attempting login.

topics.title: Offline attack
topics.definition: Attempts to crack stolen password hashes.

topics.title: Trojan
examPoints: A Trojan generally does not self-replicate.

topics.title: Reverse-connecting Trojan
topics.definition: The infected host initiates a connection to the attacker.

topics.title: Covert channel
topics.definition: Hidden communication path used to bypass security policy.

topics.title: 48. Netcat Trojan
topics.definition: Netcat can create network connections and shells.
examPoints: It is a legitimate administrative tool but can be abused for:

topics.title: 50. Wrapping
topics.definition: Wrapping combines a malicious program with a legitimate program.
examPoints: The victim sees the expected application, but malicious code also executes.

topics.title: 51. Trojan Construction Kits
examPoints: Possession or use against unauthorized systems may be illegal.

topics.title: 54. Virus vs Worm
comparisons: Attaches to a host file: Standalone malware
Often needs user action: Self-replicates automatically
Spreads through infected files: Spreads through networks

topics.title: 56. Antivirus Evasion
examPoints: Defensive focus should be on behaviour, not only signatures.

topics.title: Passive sniffing
topics.definition: Observes traffic without modifying the network.

topics.title: Active sniffing
topics.definition: Manipulates network behaviour to capture traffic.

topics.title: 60. ARP Poisoning
topics.definition: ARP poisoning sends forged ARP replies.
examPoints: The attacker may associate their MAC address with:

topics.title: Display filter
topics.definition: Decides what captured traffic is displayed.

topics.title: 62. MAC Flooding
topics.definition: MAC flooding fills a switch's CAM table with fake MAC addresses.
examPoints: The switch may start flooding frames out multiple ports.

topics.title: 63. DNS Spoofing
topics.definition: DNS spoofing causes victims to receive false DNS responses.

topics.title: DDoS
topics.definition: Multiple distributed systems attack one target.

topics.title: 68. SYN Flood
topics.definition: A SYN flood sends many SYN packets without completing the handshake.

topics.title: 69. Spoofing vs Hijacking
comparisons: Impersonates identity: Takes control of existing communication
May forge IP or MAC: Uses active session
May not require valid session: Requires session access

topics.title: 70. Session Hijacking
topics.definition: Session hijacking steals or predicts a valid session identifier.

topics.title: 74. Wireless Hacking
examPoints: Testing must be restricted to authorized wireless networks.

topics.title: WPA3
topics.definition: Introduces stronger authentication and protection against offline guessing.

topics.title: 76. SSID Discovery
topics.definition: An SSID is the wireless network name.
examPoints: Hidden SSID does not provide strong security.

topics.title: 77. MAC Spoofing
topics.definition: MAC spoofing changes the apparent MAC address.
examPoints: MAC filtering alone is not secure.

topics.title: 79. Backdoor Devices
topics.definition: A backdoor device provides unauthorized hidden access.

topics.title: Honeypot
topics.definition: Decoy system designed to attract attackers.

topics.title: Firewall
topics.definition: Controls network traffic according to rules.

topics.title: 83. Nagios
examPoints: Nagios is primarily a monitoring tool, not a full IDS.

topics.title: 85. DMZ
topics.definition: A DMZ is a separate network zone for public-facing systems.
examPoints: Reduce direct access to the internal network

topics.title: 86. Physical Security
topics.definition: Physical security protects people, buildings, hardware, and infrastructure.

topics.title: 89. Metasploit Framework
topics.definition: Metasploit is used for authorized penetration testing.

topics.title: Important terms
oneLiners: Performs scanning, enumeration, or other actions.
Metasploit should only be used in authorized labs and engagements.

topics.title: 91. Malware Families
topics.definition: A malware family is a group of related malware samples sharing:

topics.title: Static analysis
examPoints: Runtime actions may not be visible

topics.title: Dynamic analysis
topics.definition: Runs malware in a controlled environment.
examPoints: Risk if environment is not isolated

topics.title: 94. Safe Malware Analysis Environment
topics.definition: A secure malware lab should include:

topics.title: USB Pratirodh
topics.definition: Used to control and monitor USB-storage access.

topics.title: Vulnerability assessment vs penetration testing
comparisons: Lower operational risk: Higher operational risk

topics.title: Active vs passive reconnaissance
comparisons: Interacts with target: Uses public information

topics.title: Virus vs worm vs Trojan
comparisons: Attaches to host: Standalone: Disguised software
Often needs user action: Self-replicates: Requires execution
Infects files: Spreads through networks: Delivers malicious payload

topics.title: Static vs dynamic malware analysis
comparisons: Does not execute malware: Executes malware
Good for strings/imports: Good for runtime actions

topics.title: 98. Important Exam Points
oneLiners: Threat exploits vulnerability to create risk.
Active attacks modify; passive attacks observe.
Red Team attacks; Blue Team defends.
WHOIS is used for domain information.
Nmap identifies hosts, ports, services, and operating systems.
SYN scan does not complete the TCP handshake.
XMAS scan uses FIN, PSH, and URG.
NULL scan sends no TCP flags.
Password spraying uses one common password against many users.
A Trojan does not normally self-replicate.
Reverse Trojan connects from victim to attacker.
MAC flooding attacks a switch CAM table.
DNS spoofing redirects users through false DNS replies.
Hidden SSID is not a strong security control.
DMZ separates public services from internal networks.
Static malware analysis does not execute the file.
Dynamic malware analysis observes runtime behaviour.
Metasploit exploit abuses vulnerability; payload performs action.
PTES includes pre-engagement, reconnaissance, exploitation, and reporting.
Physical security is part of information security.
Cleanup is mandatory after an ethical hacking engagement.

topics.title: 99. Last-Minute Revision Sheet
workflows: Active Recon → Nmap, traceroute, banner grabbing
Online Attack → interacts with login service
Password Spraying → one password, many accounts
